coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-based nanocomposite: Long-lived thermally activated delayed fluorescence for lifetime thermal sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jiancong
Kang, Xin
Zhang, Hongyan
Liu, Yancong
Wang, Chunyan
Gao, Xinru
Li, Yaorui
coredata.dc:description: Temperature sensors with unique temperature-dependent luminescent properties have a great advantage to be used as noncontact luminescence thermometers especially in biological applications. To date, most of the photoluminescent materials for temperature sensing are based on the temperature-dependent luminescent intensity or short-lived emission lifetime, which suffer from the interference from excitation power, sample concentration, or background fluorescence. Herein, we report a carbon dot (CD)-based nanocomposite showing distinct long-lived thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) property with the lifetime of 30.85 ms at room temperature and under an air atmosphere. Notably, the TADF emission presents variable long-lived lifetimes under different temperatures, which demonstrates its promising application in lifetime thermal sensing. The interaction between the functional groups of CD and SiO2 matrix by forming Si–O–C bonds plays a key role for the synthesis process, which further improves the confinement of vibrations and rotation and harvests the triplet state efficiently. This work introduces a new perspective to prepare nanothermometers, which opens up the potential application of CD-based materials with long-lived TADF emission in highly sensitive temperature sensing with their convenience and high efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Nanocomposite
Thermally activated delayed fluorescence
Long-lived emission
Lifetime thermal sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and structure of the CD@SiO2 nanocomposite
Photoluminescent and TADF property of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite
CD@SiO2 nanocomposite for lifetime thermal sensing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
University Nursing Program for Young Scholar with Creative Talents in Heilongjiang Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed formation process of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite.
SEM and size distributions of the as-synthesized SiO2 nanospheres (A, B) and CD@SiO2 nanospheres (C, D). The size distributions are obtained by counting 50 particles.
The excitation-dependent steady-state photoluminescence spectra (A) and excitation-dependent delayed photoluminescence spectra (B) of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite.

(A) The time-resolved decay spectrum of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite at room temperature and under an air atmosphere. (B) The steady-state photoluminescence spectrum (blue line) and delayed photoluminescence spectrum (green line) of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite under excitation of 370 nm at room temperature and under an air atmosphere. (C) The steady-state photoluminescence spectrum (blue line) and delayed photoluminescence spectrum (green line) of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite under excitation of 370 nm at 77K and under an air atmosphere. (D–F) The high-resolution XPS spectra of Si 2p, O 1s, and C 1s for CD@SiO2 nanocomposite, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Temperature-responsive time-resolved decay spectra of CD@SiO2 from 243 K to 353 K. (B) Temperature-dependent emission lifetime of CD@SiO2. (C) Temperature-responsive photoluminescence spectra of CD@SiO2 from 77 K to 373 K. (D) Temperature-dependent emission intensity of CD@SiO2 (the luminescent intensities centred at 440 nm).

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-based nanocomposite: Long-lived thermally activated delayed fluorescence for lifetime thermal sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry, Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China, Heilongjiang University, Harbin, 150080, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry
Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry, Ministry of Education of the People’s Republic of China, Heilongjiang University, Harbin, 150080, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Nuclear Science and Technology, Harbin Engineering University, 145 Nantong Street, Harbin, 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Nuclear Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Nuclear Science and Technology, Harbin Engineering University, 145 Nantong Street, Harbin, 150001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Temperature sensors with unique temperature-dependent luminescent properties have a great advantage to be used as noncontact luminescence thermometers especially in biological applications. To date, most of the photoluminescent materials for temperature sensing are based on the temperature-dependent luminescent intensity or short-lived emission lifetime, which suffer from the interference from excitation power, sample concentration, or background fluorescence. Herein, we report a carbon dot (CD)-based nanocomposite showing distinct long-lived thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) property with the lifetime of 30.85 ms at room temperature and under an air atmosphere. Notably, the TADF emission presents variable long-lived lifetimes under different temperatures, which demonstrates its promising application in lifetime thermal sensing. The interaction between the functional groups of CD and SiO2 matrix by forming Si–O–C bonds plays a key role for the synthesis process, which further improves the confinement of vibrations and rotation and harvests the triplet state efficiently. This work introduces a new perspective to prepare nanothermometers, which opens up the potential application of CD-based materials with long-lived TADF emission in highly sensitive temperature sensing with their convenience and high efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: Temperature is an essential parameter in many kinds of technology and science [1]. Especially in biological events, the variation in temperature affects the biochemical reactions and physiological processes [2,3]. The accurate measurement of temperature in biological systems, such as during the hyperthermia therapy or photothermal treatment, is of great importance. Among the developed technologies for temperature sensing, noncontact luminescence thermometers with temperature-dependent emission property have shown unique advantages for biological applications [1,4]. So far, many thermometers have been developed by using their temperature-dependent luminescent intensity or dual emissions that change differently with the variable temperature [5,6]. However, the measurement of intensities for these temperature sensing materials often suffer from the interference from excitation power and sample concentration, which should be calibrated in the target environment before each use [7,8]. Lifetime-based thermal sensors can successfully overcome these problems. As the lifetimes of luminescent materials are not influenced by the sample concentration and excitation intensity in principle, it is believed the materials with efficient temperature-dependent lifetime property can serve as an ideal thermal sensor [9]. Especially for the lifetime-based thermal sensors with long-lived lifetimes, the long-lived emission might be more sensitive to temperature than traditional fluorescence, meanwhile avoiding the influence from short-lived background fluorescence effectively [10]. However, studies on the thermal sensors based on long-lived emission for efficient lifetime-based temperature sensing are still rare.

Many efforts have been devoted to synthesizing materials with long-lived emission, such as room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) or thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) materials [11–15]. Especially for TADF materials with the ability to harvest triplet excited states by the highly efficient reverse intersystem crossing process, their high quantum efficiency and unique optoelectronic property allow their applications such as lighting devices and security [16]. However, the traditional organic TADF materials often suffer from short lifetimes in the range of microseconds to several milliseconds for their easily deactivated triplet excited states by the non-radiative processes [12]. Nowadays, a new kind of carbon dot (CD)-based long-lived TADF or RTP materials have been developed with the lifetimes up to several seconds [17,18]. To achieve efficient long-lived emissions with long lifetimes, finding a suitable matrix to confine the CD to suppress the non-radiative transition caused by vibration and rotation of surface functional groups on CD is of great importance [19,20]. Recently, a “dots-in-zeolites” strategy has been successfully developed to form long-lived TADF or RTP materials by using the nanoconfined space of zeolites and complex H-bonding interaction with surface emission groups of CD to efficiently suppress the nonradiative processes and stable the triplet states [21–26]. However, the reported CD@zeolite composites are generally with the particle sizes in micrometer range, which limits their applications in biology to some extent.

Nanosized luminescent materials with good biocompatibility and safety hold great potential in biological applications [27]. Especially for the nanosized afterglow materials, the long-lived emission makes them possible to be used in high-sensitivity bioimaging and sensing [27–29]. Nanosized SiO2 spheres with good biocompatibility are suitable matrices to achieve long-lived emission materials for biological applications [30]. Besides, CD has aroused considerable attention in biological applications, such as bioimaging, biosensing, and biomedicine, due to the low biotoxicity, good biocompatibility, tunable optical properties, excellent water solubility, high sensitivity, and low cost [31–34]. Nowadays, CD, especially that with near-infrared emission, has been developed as biosensors with high sensitivity for elemental analysis in living cells through monitoring the fluorescence intensity [35]. However, the self-assembly of CD into nanosized materials remains challenging because the interaction force between CD and the inorganic matrix is weak, so the growth of nanocomposites more often suffers from phase separation [36]. Constructing interactions between the surface emissive functional groups of CD and SiO2 structure, such as covalent bonds, may efficiently stabilize the triplet excited states of CD and result in the long-lived emission of the nanocomposites [37,38]. Designing proper nanosized composites based on CD and SiO2 with long-lived temperature-dependent emission would be highly possible.

Here, a CD-based nanocomposite has been developed with the self-assembly of CD particles into nanosized SiO2 matrix by a facile hydrothermal route. The as-synthesized CD@SiO2 material shows a long TADF lifetime at ambient atmosphere, which is because the confinement of vibrations and rotation of CD functional groups with SiO2 matrix can efficiently harvests the triplet state for reverse intersystem crossing process of long-lived TADF. The temperature-dependent lifetimes of long emission has been studied, and its application in lifetime thermal sensing has been demonstrated.

All reagents were used without purification. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98%), 2-propanol (99.5%), triethylene glycol (98%), and ammonia aqueous solution (25–28%) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (C10H24N2O3, 97%) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Deionized water was got from Milli-Q purification system.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded with a Sigma-300 Zeiss scanning electron microscopy. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was taken on a FEI Tecnai G2 S-Twin F20 transmission electron microscope. Steady state photoluminescent and delayed spectra at room temperature and 77 K were measured with a HORIBA Scientific Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer. Time-resolved decay spectra to measure the lifetimes were performed at room temperature and 77 K with FluoroHub-B fluorescence lifetime system equipped with a photomultiplier tube detector in HORIBA Scientific FluoroMax-4. DAS6 software was used to fit the lifetime data with a multi-exponential model. The average lifetimes (τavg) are calculated with the equation (Ai: pre-exponential for lifetime τi):
τavg = ΣAiτi 2/ΣAiτi

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured using a Thermo ESCALAB250 spectrometer with monochromatized Al Kα excitation. Temperature-dependent time-resolved decay spectra were collected on Nanolog FL3-2iHR infrared fluorescence spectrometer. Temperature-dependent transient photoluminescence spectra were obtained with Edinburgh FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

CD was synthesized under a solvothermal condition. Typically, 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (2 mL) was added into triethylene glycol (8 mL) under stirring to form a homogeneous solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained in 180 °C for 24 h.

The CD@SiO2 nanocomposite was synthesized with a modified Stober reaction [39]. Typically, 2-propanol (31.7 mL), deionized water (12 mL), ammonia aqueous solution (6.5 mL), and the as-synthesized CD solution (2.5 mL) were mixed under 35 °C with stirring, followed by the addition of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) (0.3 mL) and stirring for 30 min. Then, more TEOS (2.5 mL) was added dropwise. The reaction mixture was kept stirring to form a homogeneous solution at 35 °C for 2 h. The resulted CD@SiO2 nanocomposite was isolated using centrifugation, washed with deionized water and ethanol, and dried in air overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structure of the CD@SiO2 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The CD-based nanocomposite is synthesized by the self-assembling of CD in the nanosized SiO2 spheres (denoted as CD@SiO2) with a modified Stober reaction under the hydrothermal condition (Fig. 1 ). The interaction between functional groups of CD and SiO2 matrix forms during the hydrolysis and condensation process of TEOS with existence of CD, avoiding the problem of phase separation. As a comparison, the traditional SiO2 nanoparticles are also prepared with the same Stober reaction condition but without adding the CD species. This facile synthetic method is also applicable for the incorporation of other quantum dots, such as perovskite quantum dots [40].

Compared with the as-synthesized traditional SiO2 nanoparticles without any luminescent property, the CD@SiO2 nanocomposite shows blue emission with ultraviolet excitation. SEM images show that the traditional SiO2 nanoparticles without CD display uniform spherical morphology with larger average diameter of 720 nm, while the as-synthesized CD@SiO2 nanospheres are with smaller average particle sizes of 590 nm (Fig. 2 ). The different sizes of the CD@SiO2 nanocomposite and SiO2 nanoparticle should be influenced by the change of pH in the synthetic system with the addition of as-synthesized CD solution [41,42]. TEM image reveals that uniform CD is embedded in the SiO2 nanoparticles with the average particle size of 1.73 nm (Fig. S1). The SiO2 matrix could effectively prevent CD from the aggregation and fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescent and TADF property of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The CD@SiO2 nanocomposite performs blue emission with the emission band centred at about 440 nm under ultraviolet excitation (Fig. 3 A). It should be noted that the photoluminescent emission of CD@SiO2 under different excitation wavelengths are with the similar centre, which is consistent with the photoluminescent behaviour of CD in the mother liquid (Fig. S2). Therefore, the excitation-independent behaviour should be due to the particular structures and photoluminescent property of CD. This further confirms that CD is embedded in the SiO2 nanoparticles by the hydrothermal process. Besides, the shift of photoluminescent emission of CD between the SiO2 matrix and mother liquid should be induced by the different environments of CD with SiO2 or solution, which is found in other CD in matrix materials [43–45].

Notably, long-lived emission is detected after turning off the excitation. The delayed emission also shows excitation-independent photoluminescent property with the similar emission wavelengths centred at about 440 nm (Fig. 3B). The delayed emission lifetime of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite at 440 nm is detected at the strongest excitation of 370 nm. The time-resolved decay spectrum shows the lifetime is 30.85 ms at room temperature and an air atmosphere (Fig. 4 A, detailed multiexponential lifetime and preexponential for lifetime are provided in Table S1). To estimate the luminescent process for the long-lived emission, the time-resolved decay spectra under room temperature and 77 K were investigated. As shown in Fig. S3, the long-lived delayed fluorescence is presented by the secondary parts of decays, while the prompt fluorescence is presented by the sharp parts of decays. It can be seen the ratio of the long-lived component in the decay at room temperature is more than that at 77 K (as shown by the arrow), which shows that the thermal energy with the increased environmental temperature can active the long-lived emission. TADF materials are characteristic with the increased long-lived emission component with the raised temperature because of the highly efficient reverse intersystem crossing process from triplet (T1) excited states to singlet (S1) excited states [11,46]. So, the long-lived emission of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite should be contributed to TADF. We further investigated the energy gap between the S1 and T1 states (ΔE ST) of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite. Fig. 4B shows that the prompt photoluminescence and delayed TADF emission are both centred at around 440 nm when excited under 370 nm at room temperature. However, when the temperature goes down to 77 K, the prompt photoluminescence is still at around 440 nm, which corresponds to the emission from S1 state. While the delayed emission moves to the centre of about 470 nm (Fig. 4C). It is known that reverse intersystem crossing process cannot perform at low temperature of 77 K. The emission of 470 nm at 77 K should corresponds to the emission from T1 state. Therefore, the ΔE ST of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite is 0.18 eV with S1 of 2.82 eV and T1 of 2.64 eV. The small ΔE ST values of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite is beneficial for the reverse intersystem crossing process from T1 to S1 at room temperature for TADF.

Considering the CD in the raw liquid shows no long-lived emission, the special long-lived TADF phenomenon should be due to the successful incorporation of CD into SiO2 matrix. XPS spectra can reveal the interaction between the functional groups of CD and SiO2 matrix and the structure of CD. As shown in Fig. 4D, the Si 2p spectrum of CD@SiO2 nanocomposite indicates the existence of Si–O–C (102.0 eV) and SiO2 (103.5 eV), which is different from the Si 2p spectrum of traditional SiO2 nanospheres (Fig. S4) with only the existence of SiO2 (103.4 eV). These prove that CD is successfully incorporated into SiO2 matrix with the formation of Si–O–C bonds [38,47]. The existence of Si–O–C (532.7 eV) and SiO2 (533.3 eV) can also be identified with the O 1s analysis (Fig. 4E). Besides, the presence of C–C/CC bonds (284.6 eV), C–O/C–N bonds (286.5 eV), and CO/CN bonds (287.8 eV) in the structure of CD are shown by the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 4F). The CO/CN bonds on the surface of the CD enable the generation of triplet states through intersystem crossing progress [48–50]. Further, the covalent bonds of Si–O–C between the functional groups with CO/CN bonds of CD and SiO2 matrix can effectively lock the vibrations and rotations of CD emission groups, thus harvest T1 state for the following reverse intersystem crossing process of long-lived TADF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD@SiO2 nanocomposite for lifetime thermal sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
Nanosized CD@SiO2 composite with temperature-dependent lifetime property based on long-lived TADF emission holds the unique advantage to be used as a high-sensitive nanothermometer. The variable long-lived lifetimes of CD@SiO2 under different temperatures are calculated according to the long-lived part of the TADF decay from 100 ms to 1400 ms. It can be seen that the lifetimes of long-lived TADF decrease with the increasing temperatures from 243 K to 353 K (Fig. 5 A). This wide temperature range enables the applications of CD@SiO2 in many fields such as electronic devices, medicine, military technology, and biological applications. The temperature-dependent lifetime can be described by the equation (Fig. 5B):
(1)
T = −38.12 × ln[(τ+0.80)/1.16 × 105]
(τ: long-lived lifetime at the sensing temperature (ms); T: sensing temperature (K))

With the clear decline in long-lived lifetimes induced by the increasing temperatures, the CD@SiO2 composite is suggested to be a potential nanothermometer for high-sensitivity lifetime thermal sensing. The shortened lifetime is mainly caused by the increased nonradiative relaxation activated by high temperatures. As shown in Table S2, the accuracy is calculated to be ±2 K. Compared with the reported nanothermometers based on luminescent intensity, this lifetime-based thermal sensor can operate without considering the intensity of excitation or the concentration of the material used for sensing. That will greatly simplify the working process of temperature sensing. Remarkably, this nanosized temperature-sensing material prolongs the long-lived emission to the lifetime millisecond timescale which shows its superior for the efficient elimination of interference from traditional short-lived background fluorescence.

The as-synthesized CD@SiO2 is also with temperature-dependent photoluminescent intensity, which furthers its application in photoluminescence intensity-based temperature sensing. We measured the variable photoluminescence intensities of CD@SiO2 under different temperatures from 77 K to 373 K (measured at the excitation of 370 nm). As shown in Fig. 5C, the luminescent intensity decreases with the rising temperature from 77 K to 163 K and from 193 K to 373 K. While at the temperature from 163 K to 193 K, the luminescent intensity rises with the rising temperature, which may be caused by the enhanced delayed fluoresce with thermal activation due to TADF phenomenon. We established the relationship between the luminescent intensities centred at 440 nm with the corresponding temperatures. As shown in Fig. 5D, the curve presents two parts of near-linear relationship under 77 K–163 K and 193 K–373 K, respectively. The relationship from 77 K to 163 K follows the equation:
(2)
T = 539.65–5.51 × 10−3 I
t

The relationship from 193 K to 373 K follows the equation:
(3)
T = 646.41–6.03 × 10−3 I
t

(I
t : luminescent intensity at the sensing temperature; T: sensing temperature (K))

The nano-sized composite with the well-known good biocompatibility and low biotoxicity, accompanied with high temperature sensitivity shows its potential to be a nanothermometer, especially for biosensing. Besides, this TADF nanocomposite with long-lived triplet state also can serve as an ideal material for gas sensing [51], ion sensing [29], and alternating-current light-emitting diode [52].

A novel CD@SiO2 nanocomposite has been synthesized with a modified Stober reaction under the hydrothermal condition. The CD is incorporated into SiO2 matrix with the connection by Si–O–C bonds, giving rise to the restriction of the vibrations and rotations of CD functional groups, and then preventing the nonradiative relaxation processes. Therefore, the triplet excited states can be efficiently stabilized, which facilitates the reverse intersystem crossing process for long-lived TADF emission with lifetime of 30.85 ms at ambient atmosphere. It should be noted that the CD@SiO2 nanocomposite shows both temperature-dependent lifetime and temperature-dependent photoluminescent intensity behaviour. Especially, the long-lived emission lifetime presents a decrease correlated with rising temperature across the range from 243 K to 353 K. The synthesized nanosized long-lived luminescent materials with unique temperature-dependent long-lived lifetime properties and good biocompatibility have great potential in lifetime-based thermal sensing application especially for biotechnological system with the advantage of their ease of use, high sensitivity of temperature, and avoidance of short-lived background fluorescence. This work may help to open more unique applications for TADF materials with long-lived emission.

Jiancong Liu: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Xin Kang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Hongyan Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yancong Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Chunyan Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xinru Gao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yaorui Li: Visualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21801069), the Fundamental Research Funds for Central Universities, China (3072019CFJ1502), the University Nursing Program for Young Scholars with Creative Talents in Heilongjiang Province, China (UNPYSCT-2018013), Heilongjiang Provincial Postdoctoral Science Foundation, China (LBH-Z18232) and the Excellent Youth Funds of Heilongjiang University, China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.108576.

Jiancong Liu conceived and designed the experiments. Xin Kang, Hongyan Zhang, Yancong Liu, Chunyan Wang, Xinru Gao, and Yaorui Li performed the synthesis and characterization. Jiancong Liu and Yaorui Li analyzed the data and wrote the paper. All the authors contributed to discussion.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent probes and sensors for temperature
